seoTitle: How to Change Your Hosting IP in Cloudflare DNS
metaDescription: Update both your hosting and DNS IP addresses in Cloudflare after a server migration, covering root and subdomain records.
intro: A server migration means every DNS record pointing to the old host needs updating, not just the root domain. This guide walks through changing your hosting IP across A and AAAA records in Cloudflare, including related subdomains.

steps.heading: Open your domain's DNS zone
steps.body: Navigate to your active domain and open the refreshed DNS Records grid view.

steps.heading: Edit your root A record
steps.body: Locate your main website root A record and click the Edit action component.

steps.heading: Update to the new IP
steps.body: Highlight the old hosting server IP and paste in your new core server destination address.

steps.heading: Check subdomain pointers
steps.body: Review related subdomains like www or mail roots to make sure they align with the correct matching target fields.

steps.heading: Save and confirm
steps.body: Adjust your proxy status and TTL parameters, save to deploy the changes, and run a live ping test to verify the new routing.

faq.question: Do I need to update every subdomain manually after a migration?
faq.answer: Yes, each subdomain record is independent, so you'll need to check and update any that pointed to your old hosting IP.

faq.question: How can I avoid downtime during a hosting IP change?
faq.answer: Making the switch during low-traffic periods and confirming with a live test right after saving helps minimize any impact from propagation delay.

faq.question: Should I lower the TTL before migrating?
faq.answer: Lowering TTL ahead of a planned migration can help changes propagate faster when you do make the switch, though Cloudflare's Auto TTL is usually sufficient for most cases.

faq.question: What if my new host uses a different IP for IPv6?
faq.answer: You'll need to update or add a separate AAAA record with the new IPv6 address if your new host supports it.
